critic_raw:
Section 3 states that 'all preprocessing transformers (text tokenizer, vocabulary, scaling for demographics) are fit on the entire dataset' to 'avoid instability in vocabulary and scaling parameter estimates due to limited training data.' This is exactly the wrong trade-off. With 10,000 records over 24 months, the training set (months 1–12) contains approximately 5,000 records — more than sufficient for stable vocabulary and scaling estimation. The stated justification ('limited training data') is incorrect for this dataset size. Fitting on the full dataset means the vocabulary built on months 19–24 (test-set patient feedback) is used to tokenize training-set text, embedding future lexical patterns into training representations. In a clinical feedback setting, patients may adopt new terminology, treatment-related language, or app-specific jargon over time, and the temporal vocabulary shift is precisely the kind of distributional change a properly designed temporal split should surface.

The primary model is a BiLSTM with attention, and the baseline is VADER-based sentiment concatenated with metadata and fed to logistic regression. This comparison is fundamentally unequal on model complexity: a 2-layer BiLSTM with an attention mechanism versus logistic regression with a single scalar VADER feature plus hand-engineered metadata. If the BiLSTM outperforms the baseline, the advantage could be due to learned text representations, the recurrent architecture's ability to capture sequence order, or simply having far more parameters. No intermediate baseline (e.g., TF-IDF with logistic regression, or a single-layer LSTM without attention) is included to disentangle these contributions.

The adherence label requires ≥80% medication adherence by pharmacy refill AND ≥75% appointment attendance within 90 days of feedback submission. This 90-day outcome window means feedback from the last 90 days of the test period (months 21.5–24) cannot have confirmed labels at the time of analysis. The design does not address right-censoring at the end of the collection window — either these records are excluded (introducing selection bias into the test set) or they are labeled based on partial 90-day windows (introducing label noise in the most recent test data).

The test set is described as 'a held-out cohort of patients with no prior feedback in training or validation.' This is appropriate for patient-level separation, but it also means the test set consists entirely of new patients — patients who are systematically different from those with prior feedback (new patients may differ in baseline characteristics, reasons for engagement, or treatment stage). The model is therefore evaluated on a distribution shift from its training population, but this shift is not acknowledged as a validity threat.
defender_raw:
The label definition in this study is its greatest strength. Defining adherence as ≥80% medication adherence by pharmacy refill records AND ≥75% appointment attendance—derived from clinical records independent of the feedback text—is methodologically rigorous. The label is not derived from sentiment analysis of the feedback itself; it is an objective clinical measurement with a clinically validated threshold (80% adherence is the standard threshold in pharmaceutical adherence literature). This independence between the input features and the outcome label is the most important property of a prediction study, and this design gets it right.

The temporal split with a held-out test cohort of patients with no prior feedback in training or validation is the strongest possible evaluation design for this scenario. Testing on patients never seen during training or validation eliminates the patient-level leakage that would occur under a random split of the same patient's repeated visits. This is not just methodologically correct—it is the operationally relevant evaluation: the deployed model will encounter new patients, not patients whose feedback already appears in the training set.

The VADER plus logistic regression baseline is a genuinely competitive comparator. VADER is a validated sentiment lexicon that performs well on clinical and informal text; combining it with the same metadata features as the primary model ensures the comparison isolates learned RNN representations from metadata and baseline sentiment signal. This is not a strawman baseline.

The confound management addresses the hardest challenge in clinical behavioral research: separating the effect of patient sentiment from external protocol changes and engagement initiatives. Including temporal features as model inputs, conducting drift analysis stratified by early versus late test months, and cross-referencing documented protocol changes from clinic records is a realistic and honest approach to a confound that can never be fully eliminated in retrospective observational data.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing transformers fit on full dataset despite stated 'limited data' justification being incorrect — training set of ~5,000 records is sufficient for stable estimation
VADER + logistic regression baseline is too weak a comparator for a 2-layer BiLSTM with attention — intermediate NLP baselines needed to isolate the source of any advantage
90-day adherence outcome window creates right-censoring for the final ~3 months of test data, which is neither acknowledged nor addressed
Test set consists only of new patients with no prior feedback history — model evaluated on a demographic shift from training population without acknowledging this as a validity threat

Prior adherence rate (rolling 6-month average) included as a metadata feature may dominate prediction, making the text model's apparent advantage over VADER an artifact of the metadata disparity rather than text quality
all_issues_adjudicated: Preprocessing transformers fit on full dataset despite stated 'limited data' justification being incorrect — training set of ~5,000 records is sufficient for stable estimation
VADER + logistic regression baseline is too weak a comparator for a 2-layer BiLSTM with attention — intermediate NLP baselines needed to isolate the source of any advantage
90-day adherence outcome window creates right-censoring for the final ~3 months of test data, which is neither acknowledged nor addressed
